findings.description: StreamingMedian.add uses bisect.insort on a flat Python list — the search is O(log n) but list.insert memmoves the tail, so each add is O(n) and n adds are O(n^2) total (a 1M-element 'stream' costs ~5e11 element shifts, i.e. minutes); replace with the classic two-heap (heapq max-half/min-half) structure for O(log n) add and O(1) median.

_data retains every value ever added with no eviction, so a long-lived pipeline stream grows memory without bound until OOM — exact median does require all points, but a component named 'Streaming' should offer a bounded window or an approximate sketch (P-squared, t-digest) for unbounded inputs.

safe_mean accepts an Iterable (inviting generators) then immediately materializes it with list(values) just to get sum and count — O(n) memory where a single-pass total/count loop is O(1), which bites when a multi-million-row pipeline generator is fed through it (~hundreds of MB of boxed floats).

Sliding-window update `current += values[i] - values[i - window]` accumulates floating-point rounding drift over long series (accuracy, not speed — Kahan summation or periodic window re-sum fixes it); noted briefly, detail deferred to critic-logic.

insort is bisect-then-insert (not atomic even under the GIL), so concurrent add() calls from pipeline workers can insert at a stale index and silently break the sorted invariant, corrupting all future medians — speculative since no concurrent caller is in scope; defer depth to critic-logic.

percentile re-sorts the input on every call, so the standard dashboard pattern of p50/p90/p99 over the same data sorts it three times — largely mitigated by Timsort being ~O(n) on already-sorted copies, hence a nit; a multi-percentile helper (or statistics.quantiles) would avoid the redundancy.
running_total hand-rolls a Python-level accumulate loop where list(itertools.accumulate(values)) does the same work at C speed — same O(n), constant-factor-only micro-optimization.
